input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-125
CAPEC Name: Flooding
CAPEC Description: An adversary consumes the resources of a target by rapidly engaging in a large number of interactions with the target. This type of attack generally exposes a weakness in rate limiting or flow. When successful this attack prevents legitimate users from accessing the service and can cause the target to crash. This attack differs from resource depletion through leaks or allocations in that the latter attacks do not rely on the volume of requests made to the target but instead focus on manipulation of the target's operations. The key factor in a flooding attack is the number of requests the adversary can make in a given period of time. The greater this number, the more likely an attack is to succeed against a given target.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2022-40188
CVE Description: Knot Resolver before 5.5.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (CPU consumption) because of algorithmic complexity. During an attack, an authoritative server must return large NS sets or address sets.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CAPEC explicitly distinguishes Flooding from algorithmic resource depletion: "This attack differs from resource depletion through... manipulation of the target's operations. The key factor in a flooding attack is the number of requests." The CVE describes Algorithmic Complexity (a few requests triggering massive internal work), not Flooding (massive volume of requests).
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] Complete mismatch based on explicit CAPEC exclusions.
</think>

Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-130
CAPEC Name: Excessive Allocation
CAPEC Description: An adversary causes the target to allocate excessive resources to servicing the attackers' request, thereby reducing the resources available for legitimate services and degrading or denying services. Usually, this attack focuses on memory allocation, but any finite resource on the target could be the attacked, including bandwidth, processing cycles, or other resources. This attack does not attempt to force this allocation through a large number of requests (that would be Resource Depletion through Flooding) but instead uses one or a small number of requests that are carefully formatted to force the target to allocate excessive resources to service this request(s). Often this attack takes advantage of a bug in the target to cause the target to allocate resources vastly beyond what would be needed for a normal request.

Evidence to Evaluate:

Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-17
CAPEC Name: Using Malicious Files
CAPEC Description: An attack of this type exploits a system's configuration that allows an adversary to either directly access an executable file, for example through shell access; or in a possible worst case allows an adversary to upload a file and then execute it. Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-663
CAPEC Name: Exploitation of Transient Instruction Execution
CAPEC Description: An adversary exploits a hardware design flaw in a CPU implementation of transient instruction execution to expose sensitive data and bypass/subvert access control over restricted resources. Typically, the adversary conducts a covert channel attack to target non-discarded microarchitectural changes caused by transient executions such as speculative execution, branch prediction, instruction pipelining, and/or out-of-order execution. The transient execution results in a series of instructions (gadgets) which construct covert channel and access/transfer the secret data.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-0549
CVE Description: Cleanup errors in some data cache evictions for some Intel(R) Processors may allow an authenticated user to potentially enable information disclosure via local access.

[Step 4: Scoring Decision] Explicit match.
</think>

Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-63
CAPEC Name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
CAPEC Description: An adversary embeds malicious scripts in content that will be served to web browsers. The goal of the attack is for the target software, the client-side browser, to execute the script with the users' privilege level. An attack of this type exploits a programs' vulnerabilities that are brought on by allowing remote hosts to execute code and scripts. Web browsers, for example, have some simple security controls in place, but if a remote attacker is allowed to execute scripts (through injecting them in to user-generated content like bulletin boards) then these controls may be bypassed. Further, these attacks are very difficult for an end user to detect.

In combination with other applications, this could lead to the absence of intended code execution.

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-1
CAPEC Name: Accessing Functionality Not Properly Constrained by ACLs
CAPEC Description: In applications, particularly web applications, access to functionality is mitigated by an authorization framework. This framework maps Access Control Lists (ACLs) to elements of the application's functionality; particularly URL's for web apps. In the case that the administrator failed to specify an ACL for a particular element, an attacker may be able to access it with impunity. An attacker with the ability to access functionality not properly constrained by ACLs can obtain sensitive information and possibly compromise the entire application. Such an attacker can access resources that must be available only to users at a higher privilege level, can access management sections of the application, or can run queries for data that they otherwise not supposed to.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-5955
CVE Description: An issue was discovered in Int15MicrocodeSmm in Insyde InsydeH2O before 2021-10-14 on Intel client chipsets. A caller may be able to escalate privileges.

CAPEC Name: Leveraging/Manipulating Configuration File Search Paths
CAPEC Description: This pattern of attack sees an adversary load a malicious resource into a program's standard path so that when a known command is executed then the system instead executes the malicious component. The adversary can either modify the search path a program uses, like a PATH variable or classpath, or they can manipulate resources on the path to point to their malicious components. J2EE applications and other component based applications that are built from multiple binaries can have very long list of dependencies to execute. If one of these libraries and/or references is controllable by the attacker then application controls can be circumvented by the attacker.

CVE-ID: CVE-2021-20661
CVE Description: Directory traversal vulnerability in SolarView Compact SV-CPT-MC310 prior to Ver.6.5 allows authenticated attackers to delete arbitrary files and/or directories on the server via unspecified vectors.

CAPEC Name: Accessing/Intercepting/Modifying HTTP Cookies
CAPEC Description: This attack relies on the use of HTTP Cookies to store credentials, state information and other critical data on client systems. There are several different forms of this attack. The first form of this attack involves accessing HTTP Cookies to mine for potentially sensitive data contained therein. The second form involves intercepting this data as it is transmitted from client to server. This intercepted information is then used by the adversary to impersonate the remote user/session. The third form is when the cookie's content is modified by the adversary before it is sent back to the server. Here the adversary seeks to convince the target server to operate on this falsified information.

Exploitation of this issue requires user interaction in that a victim must open a malicious file.

Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-586
CAPEC Name: Object Injection
CAPEC Description: An adversary attempts to exploit an application by injecting additional, malicious content during its processing of serialized objects. Developers leverage serialization in order to convert data or state into a static, binary format for saving to disk or transferring over a network. These objects are then deserialized when needed to recover the data/state. By injecting a malformed object into a vulnerable application, an adversary can potentially compromise the application by manipulating the deserialization process. This can result in a number of unwanted outcomes, including remote code execution.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-26145
CVE Description: An issue was discovered on Samsung Galaxy S3 i9305 4.4.4 devices. The WEP, WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 implementations accept second (or subsequent) broadcast fragments even when sent in plaintext and process them as full unfragmented frames. An adversary can abuse this to inject arbitrary network packets independent of the network configuration.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] Injecting network packets (Traffic Injection) at the protocol layer is completely distinct from exploiting object deserialization flaws in application code (Object Injection).
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] Complete mismatch.
</think>

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-148
CAPEC Name: Content Spoofing
CAPEC Description: An adversary modifies content to make it contain something other than what the original content producer intended while keeping the apparent source of the content unchanged. The term content spoofing is most often used to describe modification of web pages hosted by a target to display the adversary's content instead of the owner's content. However, any content can be spoofed, including the content of email messages, file transfers, or the content of other network communication protocols. Content can be modified at the source (e.g. modifying the source file for a web page) or in transit (e.g. intercepting and modifying a message between the sender and recipient). Usually, the adversary will attempt to hide the fact that the content has been modified, but in some cases, such as with web site defacement, this is not necessary. Content Spoofing can lead to malware exposure, financial fraud (if the content governs financial transactions), privacy violations, and other unwanted outcomes.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-28546
CVE Description: Acrobat Reader DC versions versions 2020.013.20074 (and earlier), 2020.001.30018 (and earlier) and 2017.011.30188 (and earlier) are missing support for an integrity check. An unauthenticated attacker could leverage this vulnerability to modify content in a certified PDF without invalidating the certification. Exploitation of this issue requires user interaction in that a victim must open the tampered file.

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-135
CAPEC Name: Format String Injection
CAPEC Description: An adversary includes formatting characters in a string input field on the target application. Most applications assume that users will provide static text and may respond unpredictably to the presence of formatting character. For example, in certain functions of the C programming languages such as printf, the formatting character %s will print the contents of a memory location expecting this location to identify a string and the formatting character %n prints the number of DWORD written in the memory. An adversary can use this to read or write to memory locations or files, or simply to manipulate the value of the resulting text in unexpected ways. Reading or writing memory may result in program crashes and writing memory could result in the execution of arbitrary code if the adversary can write to the program stack.

Evidence to Evaluate:

CVE Description: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel 5.8.9. The WEP, WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 implementations reassemble fragments even though some of them were sent in plaintext. This vulnerability can be abused to inject packets and/or exfiltrate selected fragments when another device sends fragmented frames and the WEP, CCMP, or GCMP data-confidentiality protocol is used.

CAPEC Description: Cryptanalysis is a process of finding weaknesses in cryptographic algorithms and using these weaknesses to decipher the ciphertext without knowing the secret key (instance deduction). An attacker may have other goals as well, such as: Total Break (finding the secret key), Global Deduction (finding a functionally equivalent algorithm for encryption and decryption that does not require knowledge of the secret key), Information Deduction (gaining some information about plaintexts or ciphertexts that was not previously known) and Distinguishing Algorithm (the attacker has the ability to distinguish the output of the encryption (ciphertext) from a random permutation of bits).

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-130
CAPEC Name: Excessive Allocation
CAPEC Description: An adversary causes the target to allocate excessive resources to servicing the attackers' request, thereby reducing the resources available for legitimate services and degrading or denying services. Usually, this attack focuses on memory allocation, but any finite resource on the target could be the attacked, including bandwidth, processing cycles, or other resources. This attack does not attempt to force this allocation through a large number of requests (that would be Resource Depletion through Flooding) but instead uses one or a small number of requests that are carefully formatted to force the target to allocate excessive resources to service this request(s). Often this attack takes advantage of a bug in the target to cause the target to allocate resources vastly beyond what would be needed for a normal request.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2022-3204
CVE Description: A vulnerability named 'Non-Responsive Delegation Attack' (NRDelegation Attack) has been discovered in various DNS resolving software. The NRDelegation Attack works by having a malicious delegation with a considerable number of non responsive nameservers. The attack starts by querying a resolver for a record that relies on those unresponsive nameservers. The attack can cause a resolver to spend a lot of time/resources resolving records under a malicious delegation point where a considerable number of unresponsive NS records reside. It can trigger high CPU usage in some resolver implementations that continually look in the cache for resolved NS records in that delegation. This can lead to degraded performance and eventually denial of service in orchestrated attacks. Unbound does not suffer from high CPU usage, but resources are still needed for resolving the malicious delegation. Unbound will keep trying to resolve the record until hard limits are reached. Based on the nature of the attack and the replies, different limits could be reached. From version 1.16.3 on, Unbound introduces fixes for better performance when under load, by cutting opportunistic queries for nameserver discovery and DNSKEY prefetching and limiting the number of times a delegation point can issue a cache lookup for missing records.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Excessive Allocation", where an attacker forces a target to allocate excessive resources (CPU, memory) via a small number of formatted requests, leading to a Denial of Service.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] CVE-2022-3204 describes a "Non-Responsive Delegation Attack" (NRDelegation) against DNS resolvers. An attacker sends queries for domains hosted on unresponsive nameservers, forcing the resolver to "spend a lot of time/resources resolving records," triggering "high CPU usage," and leading to "denial of service."
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The attacker crafts a specific scenario (a few requests to malicious delegations) that forces the target system to tie up its own resources (CPU and connection limits) attempting to handle the request. This perfectly maps to forcing an Excessive Allocation of system resources to cause a DoS.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] Explicit match.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE details an attack that forces a DNS resolver to excessively allocate CPU and connection resources to handle malicious delegations, directly matching the Excessive Allocation pattern.",
  "match_score": 1.0
}

CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-696
CAPEC Name: Load Value Injection
CAPEC Description: An adversary exploits a hardware design flaw in a CPU implementation of transient instruction execution in which a faulting or assisted load instruction transiently forwards adversary-controlled data from microarchitectural buffers. By inducing a page fault or microcode assist during victim execution, an adversary can force legitimate victim execution to operate on the adversary-controlled data which is stored in the microarchitectural buffers. The adversary can then use existing code gadgets and side channel analysis to discover victim secrets that have not yet been flushed from microarchitectural state or hijack the system control flow.

Evidence to Evaluate:

CVE Description: A vulnerability was discovered in Linux, FreeBSD, OpenBSD, MacOS, iOS, and Android that allows a malicious access point, or an adjacent user, to determine if a connected user is using a VPN, make positive inferences about the websites they are visiting, and determine the correct sequence and acknowledgement numbers in use, allowing the bad actor to inject data into the TCP stream. This provides everything that is needed for an attacker to hijack active connections inside the VPN tunnel.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] A network protocol vulnerability (TCP/VPN stream injection) is entirely distinct from low-level CPU microarchitectural transient execution flaws.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] Complete mismatch.

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-97
CAPEC Name: Cryptanalysis
CAPEC Description: Cryptanalysis is a process of finding weaknesses in cryptographic algorithms and using these weaknesses to decipher the ciphertext without knowing the secret key (instance deduction). Sometimes the weakness is not in the cryptographic algorithm itself, but rather in how it is applied that makes cryptanalysis successful. An attacker may have other goals as well, such as: Total Break (finding the secret key), Global Deduction (finding a functionally equivalent algorithm for encryption and decryption that does not require knowledge of the secret key), Information Deduction (gaining some information about plaintexts or ciphertexts that was not previously known) and Distinguishing Algorithm (the attacker has the ability to distinguish the output of the encryption (ciphertext) from a random permutation of bits).

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-24587
CVE Description: The 802.11 standard that underpins Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA, WPA2, and WPA3) and Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP) doesn't require that all fragments of a frame are encrypted under the same key. An adversary can abuse this to decrypt selected fragments when another device sends fragmented frames and the WEP, CCMP, or GCMP encryption key is periodically renewed.

[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] CVE-2020-24587 details a flaw in the 802.11 Wi-Fi standard where mixed-key fragmentation allows an adversary to "decrypt selected fragments."
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] Exploiting a structural design flaw in a cryptographic protocol's handling of fragments to break confidentiality and decrypt ciphertexts (Information/Instance Deduction) falls squarely under applied Cryptanalysis.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] Explicit match.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE describes exploiting a cryptographic protocol design flaw to decrypt network frames without the key, which is a practical application of Cryptanalysis.",

}

CAPEC Name: Regular Expression Exponential Blowup
CAPEC Description: An adversary may execute an attack on a program that uses a poor Regular Expression(Regex) implementation by choosing input that results in an extreme situation for the Regex. A typical extreme situation operates at exponential time compared to the input size. This is due to most implementations using a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton(NFA) state machine to be built by the Regex algorithm since NFA allows backtracking and thus more complex regular expressions.

CVE-ID: CVE-2020-27511

Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-25
CAPEC Name: Forced Deadlock
CAPEC Description: The adversary triggers and exploits a deadlock condition in the target software to cause a denial of service. A deadlock can occur when two or more competing actions are waiting for each other to finish, and thus neither ever does. Deadlock conditions can be difficult to detect.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-41141
CVE Description: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In various parts of PJSIP, when error/failure occurs, it is found that the function returns without releasing the currently held locks. This could result in a system deadlock, which cause a denial of service for the users. No release has yet been made which contains the linked fix commit. All versions up to an including 2.11.1 are affected. Users may need to manually apply the patch.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] An attacker triggering specific error/failure conditions to force the application into an unreleased lock state (deadlock) to deny service to users is the exact definition of a Forced Deadlock attack.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] Explicit match.
</think>

CAPEC Name: Path Traversal
CAPEC Description: An adversary uses path manipulation methods to exploit insufficient input validation of a target to obtain access to data that should be not be retrievable by ordinary well-formed requests. A typical variety of this attack involves specifying a path to a desired file together with dot-dot-slash characters, resulting in the file access API or function traversing out of the intended directory structure and into the root file system. By replacing or modifying the expected path information the access function or API retrieves the file desired by the attacker. These attacks either involve the attacker providing a complete path to a targeted file or using control characters (e.g. path separators (/ or \) and/or dots (.)) to reach desired directories or files.

Your goal is to determine if the vulnerability described in the CVE represents a valid instance of the overarching attack pattern described in the CAPEC.

Determine a match score: 1.0 (Strongly/Explicitly Matched), 0.5 (Implicitly/Partially Matched), or 0.0 (Mismatched/Contradicted).

You must first provide a step-by-step reasoning process enclosed strictly within <think></think> tags, covering: [Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction], [Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping], [Step 3: Alignment Analysis], and [Step 4: Scoring Decision]. After the reasoning block, output exactly one JSON object containing the "match_score".
input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-63
CAPEC Name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
CAPEC Description: An adversary embeds malicious scripts in content that will be served to web browsers. The goal of the attack is for the target software, the client-side browser, to execute the script with the users' privilege level. An attack of this type exploits a programs' vulnerabilities that are brought on by allowing remote hosts to execute code and scripts. Web browsers, for example, have some simple security controls in place, but if a remote attacker is allowed to execute scripts (through injecting them in to user-generated content like bulletin boards) then these controls may be bypassed. Further, these attacks are very difficult for an end user to detect.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-7672
CVE Description: mosc through 1.0.0 is vulnerable to Arbitrary Code Execution. User input provided to `properties` argument is executed by the `eval` function, resulting in code execution.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)", where malicious scripts are embedded in content served to client-side web browsers, executing with the user's privilege level.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes an arbitrary code execution vulnerability in "mosc" where user input is passed directly to the `eval` function.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE describes a server-side or application-level code injection flaw (Eval Injection). The CAPEC strictly focuses on client-side browser execution. These are different attack vectors and execution environments.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE details a code injection vulnerability via an 'eval' function, which mismatches the client-side browser execution mechanics defined in the Cross-Site Scripting CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-227
CAPEC Name: Sustained Client Engagement
CAPEC Description: An adversary attempts to deny legitimate users access to a resource by continually engaging a specific resource in an attempt to keep the resource tied up as long as possible. The adversary's primary goal is not to crash or flood the target, which would alert defenders; rather it is to repeatedly perform actions or abuse algorithmic flaws such that a given resource is tied up and not available to a legitimate user. By carefully crafting a requests that keep the resource engaged through what is seemingly benign requests, legitimate users are limited or completely denied access to the resource.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-40901
CVE Description: A Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDOS) vulnerability was discovered in scniro-validator v1.0.1 when validating crafted invalid emails.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Sustained Client Engagement", where an attacker ties up a resource via continuous, seemingly benign requests (like Slowloris) without aiming to crash the target, simply denying access to others.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDOS) vulnerability triggered by crafted invalid emails.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] ReDOS is an algorithmic complexity attack where a single, specifically crafted input causes the CPU to hang indefinitely evaluating a regex. This contradicts the CAPEC's pattern of using sustained, repeated benign requests to exhaust connection pools or resources.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated attack vector.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE identifies a Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDOS), an algorithmic attack triggered by a single crafted payload, which contradicts the repeated engagement strategy of the Sustained Client Engagement CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-117
CAPEC Name: Interception
CAPEC Description: An adversary monitors data streams to or from the target for information gathering purposes. This attack may be undertaken to solely gather sensitive information or to support a further attack against the target. This attack pattern can involve sniffing network traffic as well as other types of data streams (e.g. radio). The adversary can attempt to initiate the establishment of a data stream or passively observe the communications as they unfold. In all variants of this attack, the adversary is not the intended recipient of the data stream. In contrast to other means of gathering information (e.g., targeting data leaks), the adversary must actively position themself so as to observe explicit data channels (e.g. network traffic) and read the content. However, this attack differs from a Adversary-In-the-Middle (CAPEC-94) attack, as the adversary does not alter the content of the communications nor forward data to the intended recipient.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2019-5892
CVE Description: bgpd in FRRouting FRR (aka Free Range Routing) 2.x and 3.x before 3.0.4, 4.x before 4.0.1, 5.x before 5.0.2, and 6.x before 6.0.2 (not affecting Cumulus Linux or VyOS), when ENABLE_BGP_VNC is used for Virtual Network Control, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (peering session flap) via attribute 255 in a BGP UPDATE packet. This occurred during Disco in January 2019 because FRR does not implement RFC 7606, and therefore the packets with 255 were considered invalid VNC data and the BGP session was closed.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Interception", where an adversary actively positions themselves to monitor and read data streams (network traffic, radio) meant for others without altering them.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a Denial of Service vulnerability in FRRouting where sending a malformed BGP UPDATE packet (attribute 255) causes the routing session to drop (peering session flap).
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE describes an active attack sending malformed packets to crash a service (DoS). The CAPEC describes a passive intelligence-gathering attack (sniffing/monitoring).
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE describes a Denial of Service attack via malformed BGP packets, which completely contradicts the passive data monitoring and intelligence gathering described in the Interception CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-129
CAPEC Name: Pointer Manipulation
CAPEC Description: This attack pattern involves an adversary manipulating a pointer within a target application resulting in the application accessing an unintended memory location. This can result in the crashing of the application or, for certain pointer values, access to data that would not normally be possible or the execution of arbitrary code. Since pointers are simply integer variables, Integer Attacks may often be used in Pointer Attacks.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-20658
CVE Description: SolarView Compact SV-CPT-MC310 prior to Ver.6.5 allows an attacker to execute arbitrary OS commands with the web server privilege via unspecified vectors.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Pointer Manipulation", where an attacker modifies a memory pointer variable to access unintended memory locations, leading to crashes or code execution.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a vulnerability allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary OS commands with web server privileges in SolarView Compact.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE is an OS Command Injection or similar remote command execution flaw at the application level. The CAPEC is a low-level memory corruption vulnerability. There is no evidence of pointer manipulation in the CVE.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE lacks evidence for the attack pattern.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE details the execution of arbitrary OS commands at the application level, which does not map to the low-level memory addressing manipulation defined in the Pointer Manipulation CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-696
CAPEC Name: Load Value Injection
CAPEC Description: An adversary exploits a hardware design flaw in a CPU implementation of transient instruction execution in which a faulting or assisted load instruction transiently forwards adversary-controlled data from microarchitectural buffers. By inducing a page fault or microcode assist during victim execution, an adversary can force legitimate victim execution to operate on the adversary-controlled data which is stored in the microarchitectural buffers. The adversary can then use existing code gadgets and side channel analysis to discover victim secrets that have not yet been flushed from microarchitectural state or hijack the system control flow.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2019-6212
CVE Description: Multiple memory corruption issues were addressed with improved memory handling. This issue is fixed in iOS 12.1.3, tvOS 12.1.2, Safari 12.0.3, iTunes 12.9.3 for Windows, iCloud for Windows 7.10. Processing maliciously crafted web content may lead to arbitrary code execution.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Load Value Injection", a hardware design flaw in a CPU implementation involving transient instruction execution and microarchitectural buffers to hijack control flow or steal secrets.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE addresses "multiple memory corruption issues" in various Apple operating systems and software (iOS, Safari, iTunes) where processing crafted web content leads to arbitrary code execution.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE describes software-level memory corruption vulnerabilities triggered by web content. The CAPEC describes a highly specific, low-level physical CPU microarchitectural flaw. They do not match.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE describes software-level memory corruption bugs triggered by web content, which strongly contradicts the hardware-level CPU microarchitectural flaws required for Load Value Injection.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-66
CAPEC Name: SQL Injection
CAPEC Description: This attack exploits target software that constructs SQL statements based on user input. An attacker crafts input strings so that when the target software constructs SQL statements based on the input, the resulting SQL statement performs actions other than those the application intended. SQL Injection results from failure of the application to appropriately validate input.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2018-1132
CVE Description: A flaw was found in Opendaylight's SDNInterfaceapp (SDNI). Attackers can SQL inject the component's database (SQLite) without authenticating to the controller or SDNInterfaceapp. SDNInterface has been deprecated in OpenDayLight since it was last used in the final Carbon series release. In addition to the component not being included in OpenDayLight in newer releases, the SDNInterface component is not packaged in the opendaylight package included in RHEL.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The vulnerability type explicitly stated in the CVE perfectly matches the target CAPEC. The target component is a database, confirming the attack vector.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE explicitly matches the target CAPEC description.
</think>
{

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-61
CAPEC Name: Session Fixation
CAPEC Description: The attacker induces a client to establish a session with the target software using a session identifier provided by the attacker. Once the user successfully authenticates to the target software, the attacker uses the (now privileged) session identifier in their own transactions. This attack leverages the fact that the target software either relies on client-generated session identifiers or maintains the same session identifiers after privilege elevation.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2022-25896
CVE Description: This affects the package passport before 0.6.0. When a user logs in or logs out, the session is regenerated instead of being closed.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Session Fixation", where an attacker tricks a client into establishing a session using a session ID provided by the attacker, allowing the attacker to hijack the session once the user authenticates.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a flaw in the passport package where "the session is regenerated instead of being closed" when a user logs in or logs out.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The failure to properly close or rotate session identifiers upon authentication state changes (login/logout) is the fundamental root cause that enables Session Fixation attacks.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE strongly entails the exact attack pattern.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE describes a failure to properly close sessions upon login/logout, which is the exact architectural weakness that enables the Session Fixation attack pattern described in the CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 1.0
}

CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-100
CAPEC Name: Overflow Buffers
CAPEC Description: Buffer Overflow attacks target improper or missing bounds checking on buffer operations, typically triggered by input injected by an adversary. As a consequence, an adversary is able to write past the boundaries of allocated buffer regions in memory, causing a program crash or potentially redirection of execution as per the adversaries' choice.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-20653
CVE Description: Calsos CSDJ (CSDJ-B 01.08.00 and earlier, CSDJ-H 01.08.00 and earlier, CSDJ-D 01.08.00 and earlier, and CSDJ-A 03.08.00 and earlier) allows remote attackers to bypass access restriction and to obtain unauthorized historical data without access privileges via unspecified vectors.

[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a vulnerability in Calsos CSDJ that allows remote attackers to "bypass access restriction and to obtain unauthorized historical data."
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE is a logical access control or authorization failure leading to data exposure. The CAPEC is a memory-safety issue. There is no link between bypassing access restrictions to view historical data and memory buffer overflows.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE describes a logical access control bypass leading to data disclosure, which has no relationship with the memory bounds-checking failures defined in the Overflow Buffers CAPEC.",

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-663
CAPEC Name: Exploitation of Transient Instruction Execution
CAPEC Description: An adversary exploits a hardware design flaw in a CPU implementation of transient instruction execution to expose sensitive data and bypass/subvert access control over restricted resources. Typically, the adversary conducts a covert channel attack to target non-discarded microarchitectural changes caused by transient executions such as speculative execution, branch prediction, instruction pipelining, and/or out-of-order execution. The transient execution results in a series of instructions (gadgets) which construct covert channel and access/transfer the secret data.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2022-0001
CVE Description: Non-transparent sharing of branch predictor selectors between contexts in some Intel(R) Processors may allow an authorized user to potentially enable information disclosure via local access.
output:

[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE explicitly mentions a flaw in "branch predictor selectors" in Intel Processors that potentially enables "information disclosure."
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE explicitly names the exact CPU microarchitectural component (branch predictor) and the consequence (information disclosure) that define transient execution attacks (like Spectre/Meltdown), perfectly matching the CAPEC description.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE explicitly matches the target CAPEC description.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE explicitly details a hardware vulnerability involving branch predictors leading to information disclosure, which is the exact definition of the Exploitation of Transient Instruction Execution CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 1.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-35
CAPEC Name: Leverage Executable Code in Non-Executable Files
CAPEC Description: An attack of this type exploits a system's trust in configuration and resource files. When the executable loads the resource (such as an image file or configuration file) the attacker has modified the file to either execute malicious code directly or manipulate the target process (e.g. application server) to execute based on the malicious configuration parameters. Since systems are increasingly interrelated mashing up resources from local and remote sources the possibility of this attack occurring is high.

CVE Description: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.2.3. Out of bounds access exists in the functions ath6kl_wmi_pstream_timeout_event_rx and ath6kl_wmi_cac_event_rx in the file drivers/net/wireless/ath/ath6kl/wmi.c.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE describes a memory-safety vulnerability (out of bounds read/write) in C code. The CAPEC describes a logical exploitation of file trust boundaries (e.g., polyglot files or malicious configs). They are unrelated.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-127
CAPEC Name: Directory Indexing
CAPEC Description: An adversary crafts a request to a target that results in the target listing/indexing the content of a directory as output. One common method of triggering directory contents as output is to construct a request containing a path that terminates in a directory name rather than a file name since many applications are configured to provide a list of the directory's contents when such a request is received. An adversary can use this to explore the directory tree on a target as well as learn the names of files. This can often end up revealing test files, backup files, temporary files, hidden files, configuration files, user accounts, script contents, as well as naming conventions, all of which can be used by an attacker to mount additional attacks.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2022-24196
CVE Description: iText v7.1.17, up to (exluding)": 7.1.18 and 7.2.2 was discovered to contain an out-of-memory error via the component readStreamBytesRaw, which allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted PDF file.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Directory Indexing", where an attacker requests a directory path and the server responds by listing the contents of that directory, exposing sensitive files.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes an "out-of-memory error" in iText (a PDF library) when parsing a crafted PDF file, leading to a Denial of Service.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE is a resource exhaustion/memory DoS vulnerability triggered by file parsing. The CAPEC is an information disclosure vulnerability caused by web server misconfiguration.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE describes an out-of-memory denial of service during PDF parsing, which completely contradicts the server misconfiguration leading to directory file listing described in the Directory Indexing CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-227
CAPEC Name: Sustained Client Engagement
CAPEC Description: An adversary attempts to deny legitimate users access to a resource by continually engaging a specific resource in an attempt to keep the resource tied up as long as possible. The adversary's primary goal is not to crash or flood the target, which would alert defenders; rather it is to repeatedly perform actions or abuse algorithmic flaws such that a given resource is tied up and not available to a legitimate user. By carefully crafting a requests that keep the resource engaged through what is seemingly benign requests, legitimate users are limited or completely denied access to the resource.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-10995
CVE Description: PowerDNS Recursor from 4.1.0 up to and including 4.3.0 does not sufficiently defend against amplification attacks. An issue in the DNS protocol has been found that allow malicious parties to use recursive DNS services to attack third party authoritative name servers. The attack uses a crafted reply by an authoritative name server to amplify the resulting traffic between the recursive and other authoritative name servers. Both types of service can suffer degraded performance as an effect. This is triggered by random subdomains in the NSDNAME in NS records. PowerDNS Recursor 4.1.16, 4.2.2 and 4.3.1 contain a mitigation to limit the impact of this DNS protocol issue.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Sustained Client Engagement", where an attacker denies legitimate users access by tying up a specific resource using crafted or repeated requests, without necessarily aiming for an immediate crash.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a DNS amplification attack in PowerDNS Recursor triggered by crafted replies, resulting in degraded performance (resource exhaustion) for both the recursive and authoritative servers.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] A DNS amplification attack ties up the target's network bandwidth and processing capacity using crafted requests. This aligns with the CAPEC's broader definition of abusing systems to tie up a resource and limit availability for legitimate users.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE explicitly matches the target CAPEC description based on the exploitation consequence.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE details a DNS amplification attack that degrades performance by exhausting resources , which aligns with the goal of tying up resources as defined in the Sustained Client Engagement CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 1.0
}

CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-696
CAPEC Name: Load Value Injection
CAPEC Description: An adversary exploits a hardware design flaw in a CPU implementation of transient instruction execution in which a faulting or assisted load instruction transiently forwards adversary-controlled data from microarchitectural buffers. By inducing a page fault or microcode assist during victim execution, an adversary can force legitimate victim execution to operate on the adversary-controlled data which is stored in the microarchitectural buffers. The adversary can then use existing code gadgets and side channel analysis to discover victim secrets that have not yet been flushed from microarchitectural state or hijack the system control flow.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-0551
CVE Description: Load value injection in some Intel(R) Processors utilizing speculative execution may allow an authenticated user to potentially enable information disclosure via a side channel with local access. The list of affected products is provided in intel-sa-00334: https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/security-center/advisory/intel-sa-00334.html

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-158
CAPEC Name: Sniffing Network Traffic
CAPEC Description: In this attack pattern, the adversary monitors network traffic between nodes of a public or multicast network in an attempt to capture sensitive information at the protocol level. Network sniffing applications can reveal TCP/IP, DNS, Ethernet, and other low-level network communication information. The adversary takes a passive role in this attack pattern and simply observes and analyzes the traffic. The adversary may precipitate or indirectly influence the content of the observed transaction, but is never the intended recipient of the target information.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-8897
CVE Description: A weak robustness vulnerability exists in the AWS Encryption SDKs for Java, Python, C and Javalcript prior to versions 2.0.0. Due to the non-committing property of AES-GCM (and other AEAD ciphers such as AES-GCM-SIV or (X)ChaCha20Poly1305) used by the SDKs to encrypt messages, an attacker can craft a unique cyphertext which will decrypt to multiple different results, and becomes especially relevant in a multi-recipient setting. We recommend users update their SDK to 2.0.0 or later.

[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a "weak robustness vulnerability" in AWS Encryption SDKs where the non-committing property of AES-GCM allows an attacker to craft unique ciphertext that decrypts to multiple results.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE focuses on a cryptographic flaw requiring active crafting of malicious ciphertext. The CAPEC focuses on passive observation of network packets. The methodologies do not overlap.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated attack vector.
</think>

}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-1
CAPEC Name: Accessing Functionality Not Properly Constrained by ACLs
CAPEC Description: In applications, particularly web applications, access to functionality is mitigated by an authorization framework. This framework maps Access Control Lists (ACLs) to elements of the application's functionality; particularly URL's for web apps. In the case that the administrator failed to specify an ACL for a particular element, an attacker may be able to access it with impunity. An attacker with the ability to access functionality not properly constrained by ACLs can obtain sensitive information and possibly compromise the entire application. Such an attacker can access resources that must be available only to users at a higher privilege level, can access management sections of the application, or can run queries for data that they otherwise not supposed to.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-7640
CVE Description: pixl-class prior to 1.0.3 allows execution of arbitrary commands. The members argument of the create function can be controlled by users without any sanitization.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE describes an OS command injection or arbitrary code execution flaw arising from improper input sanitization. The CAPEC describes a logical flaw where access control checks are missing. These are distinct vulnerability classes.

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-129
CAPEC Name: Pointer Manipulation
CAPEC Description: This attack pattern involves an adversary manipulating a pointer within a target application resulting in the application accessing an unintended memory location. This can result in the crashing of the application or, for certain pointer values, access to data that would not normally be possible or the execution of arbitrary code. Since pointers are simply integer variables, Integer Attacks may often be used in Pointer Attacks.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-34852
CVE Description: This vulnerability allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected installations of Foxit PDF Reader 11.0.0.49893. User interaction is required to exploit this vulnerability in that the target must visit a malicious page or open a malicious file. The specific flaw exists within the handling of Annotation objects. The issue results from the lack of validating the existence of an object prior to performing operations on the object. An attacker can leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current process. Was ZDI-CAN-13929.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Pointer Manipulation", where an adversary manipulates a pointer to access unintended memory locations, potentially crashing the application or executing arbitrary code.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a flaw in Foxit PDF Reader where the application performs operations on Annotation objects without "validating the existence of an object," allowing remote code execution.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] Operating on an object without validating its existence usually implies a Use-After-Free (UAF) or Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability, both of which fundamentally rely on evaluating or manipulating an invalid pointer in memory to achieve execution.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE strongly entails the attack pattern.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE describes operating on objects without validating their existence, a memory corruption flaw (like Use-After-Free) that fundamentally relies on Pointer Manipulation to achieve the stated arbitrary code execution.",
  "match_score": 1.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-1
CAPEC Name: Accessing Functionality Not Properly Constrained by ACLs
CAPEC Description: In applications, particularly web applications, access to functionality is mitigated by an authorization framework. This framework maps Access Control Lists (ACLs) to elements of the application's functionality; particularly URL's for web apps. In the case that the administrator failed to specify an ACL for a particular element, an attacker may be able to access it with impunity. An attacker with the ability to access functionality not properly constrained by ACLs can obtain sensitive information and possibly compromise the entire application. Such an attacker can access resources that must be available only to users at a higher privilege level, can access management sections of the application, or can run queries for data that they otherwise not supposed to.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2022-26941
CVE Description: A format string vulnerability exists in Motorola MTM5000 series firmware AT command handler for the AT+CTGL command. An attacker-controllable string is improperly handled, allowing for a write-anything-anywhere scenario. This can be leveraged to obtain arbitrary code execution inside the teds_app binary, which runs with root privileges.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Accessing Functionality Not Properly Constrained by ACLs", targeting authorization frameworks to bypass access control lists and view or modify unauthorized data.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE identifies a "format string vulnerability" in Motorola firmware allowing an attacker to achieve a "write-anything-anywhere scenario" and gain arbitrary code execution as root.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE describes a severe memory safety vulnerability (format string injection). The CAPEC describes a logical flaw regarding missing authorization checks. These are distinct vulnerability types.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE details a memory-safety format string vulnerability leading to root execution, which has no relationship with the logical authorization bypasses described in the target CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-125
CAPEC Name: Flooding
CAPEC Description: An adversary consumes the resources of a target by rapidly engaging in a large number of interactions with the target. This type of attack generally exposes a weakness in rate limiting or flow. When successful this attack prevents legitimate users from accessing the service and can cause the target to crash. This attack differs from resource depletion through leaks or allocations in that the latter attacks do not rely on the volume of requests made to the target but instead focus on manipulation of the target's operations. The key factor in a flooding attack is the number of requests the adversary can make in a given period of time. The greater this number, the more likely an attack is to succeed against a given target.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-31441
CVE Description: This vulnerability allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected installations of Foxit Reader 10.1.1.37576. User interaction is required to exploit this vulnerability in that the target must visit a malicious page or open a malicious file. The specific flaw exists within the handling of Annotation objects. The issue results from the lack of validating the existence of an object prior to performing operations on the object. An attacker can leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current process. Was ZDI-CAN-13101.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Flooding", where an adversary consumes target resources by rapidly engaging in a massive volume of interactions, causing a denial of service.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes an RCE vulnerability in Foxit Reader due to improper handling of Annotation objects (lack of existence validation) upon opening a malicious file.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE describes a memory corruption flaw triggered by parsing a single malicious file to achieve code execution. The CAPEC describes a volumetric network attack designed to exhaust resources. They do not match.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE details a memory corruption vulnerability resulting in code execution from a single malicious file, completely mismatching the volumetric, high-request-rate mechanics of the Flooding CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

Target CAPEC:

CAPEC Name: Leveraging/Manipulating Configuration File Search Paths
CAPEC Description: This pattern of attack sees an adversary load a malicious resource into a program's standard path so that when a known command is executed then the system instead executes the malicious component. The adversary can either modify the search path a program uses, like a PATH variable or classpath, or they can manipulate resources on the path to point to their malicious components. J2EE applications and other component based applications that are built from multiple binaries can have very long list of dependencies to execute. input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-490
CAPEC Name: Amplification
CAPEC Description: An adversary may execute an amplification where the size of a response is far greater than that of the request that generates it. The goal of this attack is to use a relatively few resources to create a large amount of traffic against a target server. To execute this attack, an adversary send a request to a 3rd party service, spoofing the source address to be that of the target server. The larger response that is generated by the 3rd party service is then sent to the target server. By sending a large number of initial requests, the adversary can generate a tremendous amount of traffic directed at the target. The greater the discrepancy in size between the initial request and the final payload delivered to the target increased the effectiveness of this attack.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-12662
CVE Description: Unbound before 1.10.1 has Insufficient Control of Network Message Volume, aka an "NXNSAttack" issue. This is triggered by random subdomains in the NSDNAME in NS records.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] NXNSAttack is a highly specific, well-documented DNS Amplification attack where an attacker delegates to a malicious authoritative server that returns a massive list of NS records, forcing the recursive resolver to amplify traffic. This perfectly matches the Amplification CAPEC.

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-130
CAPEC Name: Excessive Allocation
CAPEC Description: An adversary causes the target to allocate excessive resources to servicing the attackers' request, thereby reducing the resources available for legitimate services and degrading or denying services. Usually, this attack focuses on memory allocation, but any finite resource on the target could be the attacked, including bandwidth, processing cycles, or other resources. This attack does not attempt to force this allocation through a large number of requests (that would be Resource Depletion through Flooding) but instead uses one or a small number of requests that are carefully formatted to force the target to allocate excessive resources to service this request(s). Often this attack takes advantage of a bug in the target to cause the target to allocate resources vastly beyond what would be needed for a normal request.

Evidence to Evaluate:

CVE Description: iText v7.1.17, up to (exluding)": 7.1.18 and 7.2.2 was discovered to contain an out-of-memory error via the component readStreamBytesRaw, which allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted PDF file.

CAPEC Description: Cryptanalysis is a process of finding weaknesses in cryptographic algorithms and using these weaknesses to decipher the ciphertext without knowing the secret key (instance deduction). Sometimes the weakness is not in the cryptographic algorithm itself, but rather in how it is applied that makes cryptanalysis successful. An attacker may have other goals as well, such as: Total Break (finding the secret key), Global Deduction (finding a functionally equivalent algorithm for encryption and decryption that does not require knowledge of the secret key), Information Deduction (gaining some information about plaintexts or ciphertexts that was not previously known) and Distinguishing Algorithm (the attacker has the ability to distinguish the output of the encryption (ciphertext) from a random permutation of bits).

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-77
CAPEC Name: Manipulating User-Controlled Variables
CAPEC Description: This attack targets user controlled variables (DEBUG=1, PHP Globals, and So Forth). An adversary can override variables leveraging user-supplied, untrusted query variables directly used on the application server without any data sanitization. In extreme cases, the adversary can change variables controlling the business logic of the application. For instance, in languages like PHP, a number of poorly set default configurations may allow the user to override variables.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2022-24760
CVE Description: Parse Server is an open source http web server backend. In versions prior to 4.10.7 there is a Remote Code Execution (RCE) vulnerability in Parse Server. This vulnerability affects Parse Server in the default configuration with MongoDB. The main weakness that leads to RCE is the Prototype Pollution vulnerable code in the file `DatabaseController.js`, so it is likely to affect Postgres and any other database backend as well. This vulnerability has been confirmed on Linux (Ubuntu) and Windows. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. The only known workaround is to manually patch your installation with code referenced at the source GHSA-p6h4-93qp-jhcm.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] Prototype pollution relies on injecting properties into base objects (like `Object.prototype` in JavaScript) via untrusted input. This fundamentally manipulates the environment's internal variables and properties, aligning perfectly with the concept of manipulating user-controlled variables to alter business logic.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE strongly entails the target CAPEC description.
</think>

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-1
CAPEC Name: Accessing Functionality Not Properly Constrained by ACLs
CAPEC Description: In applications, particularly web applications, access to functionality is mitigated by an authorization framework. This framework maps Access Control Lists (ACLs) to elements of the application's functionality; particularly URL's for web apps. In the case that the administrator failed to specify an ACL for a particular element, an attacker may be able to access it with impunity. An attacker with the ability to access functionality not properly constrained by ACLs can obtain sensitive information and possibly compromise the entire application. Such an attacker can access resources that must be available only to users at a higher privilege level, can access management sections of the application, or can run queries for data that they otherwise not supposed to.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-20657
CVE Description: Improper access control vulnerability in SolarView Compact SV-CPT-MC310 prior to Ver.6.5 allows an authenticated attacker to obtain and/or alter the setting information without the access privilege via unspecified vectors.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] Altering settings "without the access privilege" due to "improper access control" is the exact definition of accessing functionality not properly constrained by ACLs.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE explicitly matches the target CAPEC description.

CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-129
CAPEC Name: Pointer Manipulation
CAPEC Description: This attack pattern involves an adversary manipulating a pointer within a target application resulting in the application accessing an unintended memory location. This can result in the crashing of the application or, for certain pointer values, access to data that would not normally be possible or the execution of arbitrary code. Since pointers are simply integer variables, Integer Attacks may often be used in Pointer Attacks.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-31457
CVE Description: This vulnerability allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected installations of Foxit Reader 10.1.1.37576. User interaction is required to exploit this vulnerability in that the target must visit a malicious page or open a malicious file. The specific flaw exists within the handling of Annotation objects. The issue results from the lack of validating the existence of an object prior to performing operations on the object. An attacker can leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current process. Was ZDI-CAN-13147.

Processing maliciously crafted web content may lead to arbitrary code execution.

CAPEC Description: This attack targets a race condition occurring between the time of check (state) for a resource and the time of use of a resource. A typical example is file access. The adversary can leverage a file access race condition by "running the race", meaning that they would modify the resource between the first time the target program accesses the file and the time the target program uses the file. During that period of time, the adversary could replace or modify the file, causing the application to behave unexpectedly.

Evidence to Evaluate:

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-25
CAPEC Name: Forced Deadlock
CAPEC Description: The adversary triggers and exploits a deadlock condition in the target software to cause a denial of service. A deadlock can occur when two or more competing actions are waiting for each other to finish, and thus neither ever does. Deadlock conditions can be difficult to detect.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2019-17212
CVE Description: Buffer overflows were discovered in the CoAP library in Arm Mbed OS 5.14.0. The CoAP parser is responsible for parsing received CoAP packets. The function sn_coap_parser_options_parse() parses CoAP input linearly using a while loop. Once an option is parsed in a loop, the current point (*packet_data_pptr) is increased correspondingly. The pointer is restricted by the size of the received buffer, as well as by the 0xFF delimiter byte. Inside each while loop, the check of the value of *packet_data_pptr is not strictly enforced. More specifically, inside a loop, *packet_data_pptr could be increased and then dereferenced without checking. Moreover, there are many other functions in the format of sn_coap_parser_****() that do not check whether the pointer is within the bounds of the allocated buffer. All of these lead to heap-based or stack-based buffer overflows, depending on how the CoAP packet buffer is allocated.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Forced Deadlock", an attack where an adversary triggers a deadlock condition (competing actions waiting for each other infinitely) to cause a denial of service.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE details "Buffer overflows" in a CoAP parser due to pointer manipulation without strictly enforcing boundary checks within a `while` loop.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE describes a spatial memory safety vulnerability (buffer overflow). The CAPEC describes a concurrent execution state failure (deadlock). These are entirely unrelated software flaws.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE explicitly identifies a buffer overflow caused by missing bounds checks, which completely contradicts the concurrency-based infinite waiting states defined in the Forced Deadlock CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-124
CAPEC Name: Shared Resource Manipulation
CAPEC Description: An adversary exploits a resource shared between multiple applications, an application pool or hardware pin multiplexing to affect behavior. Resources may be shared between multiple applications or between multiple threads of a single application. Resource sharing is usually accomplished through mutual access to a single memory location or multiplexed hardware pins. If an adversary can manipulate this shared resource (usually by co-opting one of the applications or threads) the other applications or threads using the shared resource will often continue to trust the validity of the compromised shared resource and use it in their calculations. This can result in invalid trust assumptions, corruption of additional data through the normal operations of the other users of the shared resource, or even cause a crash or compromise of the sharing applications.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-20659
CVE Description: SolarView Compact SV-CPT-MC310 prior to Ver.6.5 allows an authenticated attacker to upload arbitrary files via unspecified vectors. If the file is PHP script, an attacker may execute arbitrary code.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] Arbitrary file upload is a web application logic and input validation flaw. It does not relate to the manipulation of concurrent execution or shared memory resources.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-62
CAPEC Name: Cross Site Request Forgery
CAPEC Description: An attacker crafts malicious web links and distributes them (via web pages, email, etc.), typically in a targeted manner, hoping to induce users to click on the link and execute the malicious action against some third-party application. If successful, the action embedded in the malicious link will be processed and accepted by the targeted application with the users' privilege level. This type of attack leverages the persistence and implicit trust placed in user session cookies by many web applications today. In such an architecture, once the user authenticates to an application and a session cookie is created on the user's system, all following transactions for that session are authenticated using that cookie including potential actions initiated by an attacker and simply "riding" the existing session cookie.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-10995
CVE Description: PowerDNS Recursor from 4.1.0 up to and including 4.3.0 does not sufficiently defend against amplification attacks. An issue in the DNS protocol has been found that allow malicious parties to use recursive DNS services to attack third party authoritative name servers. The attack uses a crafted reply by an authoritative name server to amplify the resulting traffic between the recursive and other authoritative name servers. Both types of service can suffer degraded performance as an effect. This is triggered by random subdomains in the NSDNAME in NS records. PowerDNS Recursor 4.1.16, 4.2.2 and 4.3.1 contain a mitigation to limit the impact of this DNS protocol issue.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Cross Site Request Forgery", leveraging a user's authenticated session cookie by tricking them into clicking a link that performs an action on a trusted web application.
[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE describes a "DNS protocol issue" in PowerDNS Recursor allowing "amplification attacks" to degrade performance via crafted replies.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE is a network infrastructure layer denial-of-service attack (DNS Amplification). The CAPEC is an application-layer web session riding attack. They are mutually exclusive.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE describes an entirely unrelated vulnerability.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE details a DNS amplification attack targeting network infrastructure, which entirely contradicts the web application session riding mechanics of the Cross Site Request Forgery CAPEC.",
  "match_score": 0.0
}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-1
CAPEC Name: Accessing Functionality Not Properly Constrained by ACLs
CAPEC Description: In applications, particularly web applications, access to functionality is mitigated by an authorization framework. This framework maps Access Control Lists (ACLs) to elements of the application's functionality; particularly URL's for web apps. In the case that the administrator failed to specify an ACL for a particular element, an attacker may be able to access it with impunity. An attacker with the ability to access functionality not properly constrained by ACLs can obtain sensitive information and possibly compromise the entire application. Such an attacker can access resources that must be available only to users at a higher privilege level, can access management sections of the application, or can run queries for data that they otherwise not supposed to.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2022-25896
CVE Description: This affects the package passport before 0.6.0. When a user logs in or logs out, the session is regenerated instead of being closed.

[Step 2: CVE Vulnerability Mapping] The CVE states that the `passport` package regenerates sessions instead of closing them when a user logs in or out.
[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] The CVE is a session management flaw (enabling Session Fixation). The CAPEC focuses on missing authorization checks (ACLs) for specific URLs or functions. These are distinct vulnerability domains.

There are no known workarounds and users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible.

CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-492
CAPEC Name: Regular Expression Exponential Blowup
CAPEC Description: An adversary may execute an attack on a program that uses a poor Regular Expression(Regex) implementation by choosing input that results in an extreme situation for the Regex. A typical extreme situation operates at exponential time compared to the input size. This is due to most implementations using a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton(NFA) state machine to be built by the Regex algorithm since NFA allows backtracking and thus more complex regular expressions.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-3765
CVE Description: validator.js is vulnerable to Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-97
CAPEC Name: Cryptanalysis
CAPEC Description: Cryptanalysis is a process of finding weaknesses in cryptographic algorithms and using these weaknesses to decipher the ciphertext without knowing the secret key (instance deduction). Sometimes the weakness is not in the cryptographic algorithm itself, but rather in how it is applied that makes cryptanalysis successful. An attacker may have other goals as well, such as: Total Break (finding the secret key), Global Deduction (finding a functionally equivalent algorithm for encryption and decryption that does not require knowledge of the secret key), Information Deduction (gaining some information about plaintexts or ciphertexts that was not previously known) and Distinguishing Algorithm (the attacker has the ability to distinguish the output of the encryption (ciphertext) from a random permutation of bits).

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-8897
CVE Description: A weak robustness vulnerability exists in the AWS Encryption SDKs for Java, Python, C and Javalcript prior to versions 2.0.0. Due to the non-committing property of AES-GCM (and other AEAD ciphers such as AES-GCM-SIV or (X)ChaCha20Poly1305) used by the SDKs to encrypt messages, an attacker can craft a unique cyphertext which will decrypt to multiple different results, and becomes especially relevant in a multi-recipient setting. We recommend users update their SDK to 2.0.0 or later.
output:
<think>
[Step 1: CAPEC Profile Extraction] CAPEC describes "Cryptanalysis", focusing on exploiting weaknesses in cryptographic algorithms or their specific implementations/applications to decipher ciphertext or achieve other attacker goals.

[Step 3: Alignment Analysis] Exploiting the mathematical or property-based weaknesses in how AES-GCM is applied (the non-committing property) to achieve unauthorized cryptographic outcomes is a direct instance of Cryptanalysis.
[Step 4: Scoring Decision] The CVE explicitly matches the target CAPEC description.
</think>
{
  "evaluation_summary": "The CVE details exploiting a mathematical property (non-committing) of the AES-GCM cipher application, perfectly matching the algorithm weakness exploitation described in the Cryptanalysis CAPEC.",

}

input:
Target CAPEC:
CAPEC-ID: CAPEC-130
CAPEC Name: Excessive Allocation
CAPEC Description: An adversary causes the target to allocate excessive resources to servicing the attackers' request, thereby reducing the resources available for legitimate services and degrading or denying services. Usually, this attack focuses on memory allocation, but any finite resource on the target could be the attacked, including bandwidth, processing cycles, or other resources. This attack does not attempt to force this allocation through a large number of requests (that would be Resource Depletion through Flooding) but instead uses one or a small number of requests that are carefully formatted to force the target to allocate excessive resources to service this request(s). Often this attack takes advantage of a bug in the target to cause the target to allocate resources vastly beyond what would be needed for a normal request.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2020-7623
CVE Description: jscover through 1.0.0 is vulnerable to Command Injection. It allows execution of arbitrary command via the source argument.

CAPEC Name: Regular Expression Exponential Blowup
CAPEC Description: An adversary may execute an attack on a program that uses a poor Regular Expression(Regex) implementation by choosing input that results in an extreme situation for the Regex. A typical extreme situation operates at exponential time compared to the input size. This is due to most implementations using a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton(NFA) state machine to be built by the Regex algorithm since NFA allows backtracking and thus more complex regular expressions.

Evidence to Evaluate:
CVE-ID: CVE-2021-40900
CVE Description: A Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDOS) vulnerability was discovered in regexfn v1.0.5 when validating crafted invalid emails.
